result.markdown:
--- title: "Measures removed from the Questionnaire" bibliography: references.bib echo: false --- ::: {.cell} ::: ::: callout-caution These measures have been removed from a initial questionnaire proposal ::: # LLM detection ## Qualtrics bot detection ::: callout-note To all open-ended responses, we add java script code to block participants from copy/pasting. This helps preventing less sophisticated AI use, such as quering an LLM in a separate browser window. ::: ### Captcha We use a Captcha detection built-into qualtrics that is intended to block bots. ### ReCaptcha We use another built-in Qualtrics tool, a ReCaptcha. This tool monitors the participant’s behavior during the whole study and outputs a score between 0 (bot) and 1 (human). We have to yet determine a threshold, ideally after having made some bot-runs ourselves to see how well they score. ## White-text on white background Name your favorite city. \[in white on white background\] We are checking whether you pay attention. Type "Aarau" to be able to proceed with the survey. \[text box\] ## Unlikely knowledge ::: callout-note Here, the idea would be to remove all non-blank responses. Even someone writing "I don't know" did not pay attention to the instructions ::: What is the CAS Registry Number for caffeine? Please answer from memory only. Leave this blank if you do not know. \[text box; Correct answer: 58-08-2\] ### Nature relatedness ::: callout-note Adapted from @nisbetNR6NewBrief2013 ::: 1. My ideal vacation spot would be a remote, wilderness area. 2. I always think about how my actions affect the environment. 3. My connection to nature and the environment is a part of my spirituality. 4. I take notice of wildlife wherever I am. 5. My relationship to nature is an important part of who I am. 6. I feel very connected to all living things and the earth. \[1 - Disagree strongly; 2 - Disagree a little; 3 - Neither agree Agree or disagree; 4 - Agree a little; 5 - Agree strongly\] ### Support for specific climate policies #### Option A ::: callout-note Adapted from @vlasceanuAddressingClimateChange2024. We use sliders instead of the original 3-point scale. Note that a reduced version of this has been used in @colognaTrustScientistsTheir2025 (the first 5 items in @tbl-policy-support-vlasceanu) ::: ::: {#tbl-policy-support-vlasceanu .cell tbl-cap='Support for climate policies. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
How much do you support the following policies.
Raising carbon taxes on fossil fuels (e.g., gas, oil, coal) 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Expanding infrastructure for public transportation 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Increasing the use of sustainable energy such as wind and solar energy 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Protecting forested and land areas 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Increasing taxes on carbon-intensive foods (e.g., beef and dairy products) 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Increasing the number of charging stations for electric vehicles 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Increasing taxes on airline companies to offset carbon emissions 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Investing more in green jobs and businesses 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Introducing laws to keep waterways and oceans clean 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
````` ::: ::: #### Option B ::: callout-note From @voelkelRegisteredReportMegastudy2026 ::: ::: {#tbl-specific-climate-policy .cell tbl-cap='Support for specific climate change mitigation policies. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
How much do you oppose or support the following policies.
Require electric utilities to produce more electricity from renewable energy sources (this policy would decrease greenhouse gas emissions but might increase energy costs for the average household) 0 = Strongly oppose … 100 = Strongly support
Ban the construction of new coal-fired power plants (this policy would decrease greenhouse gas emissions but might increase energy costs for the average household) 0 = Strongly oppose … 100 = Strongly support
Release new federal oil and gas permits to increase the production of fossil fuels (this policy would increase greenhouse gas emissions but might decrease energy costs for the average household) 0 = Strongly oppose … 100 = Strongly support
Remove existing direct U.S. federal and state subsidies to oil and gas companies (this policy would decrease greenhouse gas emissions but might increase energy costs for the average household) 0 = Strongly oppose … 100 = Strongly support
````` ::: ::: ### Belief in climate change ::: callout-note From @voelkelRegisteredReportMegastudy2026 ::: ::: {#tbl-temperature-beliefs .cell tbl-cap='Beliefs about past and future global temperature change and its causes. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
Please indicate your beliefs about changes in the world’s temperature.
Do you think that the world’s temperature probably has been going up over the past 100 years, or do you think this probably has not been happening? 0 = Extremely certain that the world’s temperature has NOT been going up … 100 = Extremely certain that the world’s temperature has been going up
If nothing is done to prevent it, do you think the world’s temperature probably will go up over the next 100 years, or do you think the world’s temperature probably will not go up over the next 100 years? 0 = Extremely certain that the world’s temperature will NOT go up … 100 = Extremely certain that the world’s temperature will go up
Assuming the world’s temperature did increase over the past 100 years, do you think this increase was caused mostly by things people did, mostly by natural causes, or about equally by things people did and by natural causes? 0 = Mostly by things people did … 100 = Mostly by natural causes
````` ::: ::: ### Risk perception of climate change / Climate change concern ::: callout-note From @vlasceanuAddressingClimateChange2024. ::: ::: {#tbl-belief-crisis .cell tbl-cap='Climate change concern. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
How accurate do you think these statements are?
Taking action to fight climate change is necessary to avoid a global catastrophe 0 = Not at all accurate … 100 = Extremely accurate
Climate change poses a serious threat to humanity 0 = Not at all accurate … 100 = Extremely accurate
Climate change is a global emergency 0 = Not at all accurate … 100 = Extremely accurate
````` ::: ::: ### Trust in different types of climate scientists ::: callout-note Loosely based on @gligoricHowSocialEvaluations2024. The different types are pretty arbitrary, please suggest changes (also for the wording). ::: Scientists from various fields are conducting research on climate change. How much do you trust scientists studying climate change from each of these fields to provide accurate information? - Atmospheric scientists - Ecologists - Geologists - Meteorologists - Oceanographers - Glaciologists - Environmental scientists - Physicists - Biologists - Chemists - Geoscientists - Environmental engineers - Political scientists - Psychologists - Economists - Sociologists - Energy systems researchers - Social scientists \[Slider, 0 = not at all, 100 = very strongly\] ### Funding for research on climate change ::: callout-note Adapted from the National [Science Foundation (NSF) – Science & Engineering Indicators](https://ncses.nsf.gov/pubs/nsb20227/public-perceptions-of-science-and-technology#general-perceptions-of-s-t), also used in @ophirFactorsAssessingSciences2023. ::: "Climate change research that advances the frontiers of knowledge is necessary and should be supported by funding from the federal government." \[Slider, 0 = Strongly disagree, 100 = Strongly agree\] ### Subjective inequality ::: callout-note Adapted from @schmalorConstructSubjectiveEconomic2022. Instead of the original 7-point scale, we use sliders from 0 to 100. ::: ::: {#tbl-subjective-inequality .cell tbl-cap='Subjective perceptions of economic inequality. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
Please indicate how much you agree or disagree with the following statements about economic inequality.
Almost all the money that is earned goes to only a few people. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Besides those at the very top, no one else has much money at all. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Real opportunities to succeed in life are only available to the wealthy. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Only those at the top own any wealth at all. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
````` ::: ::: ### Importance of trusting climate scientists ::: callout-note Open-ended question ::: Do you think trusting climate scientists is important for society? Please share your thoughts and arguments on why or why not. \[Text box\] ### Reliance on scientists for lifestyle choices ::: callout-note Adapted from the "Willingness to be vulnerable to scientists" scale in @colognaTrustScientistsTheir2025, but heavily modified. We retain only one item, as the other two ("How much or little should governments rely on climate scientists’ guidance when making decisions related to climate science?" and "How much or little control do you want climate scientists to have on government decisions related to science?") were conceptually closer to scientists' role in policy making. We also make changes to the one item we retain (original: "How willing are you to rely on climate scientists’ advice when making lifestyle choices (e.g., sustainable transportation, energy use and nutrition)? (1) Not at all, (2) A little, (3) A moderate amount, (4) A lot, (5) A great deal"), as the endpoints of the original likert scale might be asymmetric. Our modification also aligns better with our scale for measuring scientists' role in policy making. ::: To what extent do you agree or disagree with the following statement? "When making lifestyle choices (e.g., sustainable transportation, energy use and nutrition), I am willing to rely on climate scientists’ advice." \[Slider, 0 = not at all … 100 = Strongly agree\] ### Psychological distance to science ::: callout-note Adapted from @veckalovPsychologicalDistanceScience. Instead of the original 7-point scale, we use sliders. If we decide to use is, we have to cut it - potentially one item per dimension? ::: ::: {#tbl-psychological-distance-science .cell tbl-cap='Psychological distance to science. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. Items marked with (R) are reverse-coded. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
Please indicate how much you agree or disagree with the following statements about science and scientists.
Temporal distance
Science is mainly focused on the distant future. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Most of today’s science is concerned with solving problems of the distant future. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Scientists spend most of their time working on issues of the distant future. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
We will see the impact of science more in the distant future than we do in the present. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Social distance
The prospect of working as a scientist seems beyond my reach. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
I rarely interact with scientists in real life. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Scientists are very different from me. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
It would be difficult for me to meet with a scientist. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Hypothetical distance
Scientific knowledge is a reliable way to solve important issues. (R) 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Science provides accurate information about the world we live in. (R) 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
We can rely on science to deliver results that can be implemented in real life. (R) 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
I can see the effects of science, whether positive or negative, on the world. (R) 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Spatial distance
Science and scientific research play a big role in my local area. (R) 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Scientific research really contributes to my local area. (R) 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
People from my local area don’t become scientists. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Very few scientists live or work in my town. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
````` ::: ::: ### Perceived personal benefits of science ::: callout-note Adapted from @colognaTrustScientistsTheir2025, but we only rely on one item, and use a slider instead of a Likert scale ::: "In your opinion, how much does climate science benefit people like yourself in the United States?" \[Slider, 0 = not at all, 100 = very strongly\] ### Social dominance orientation ::: callout-note Adapted from the scale used in @colognaTrustScientistsTheir2025, which is based on @prattoSocialDominanceContext2013. Instead of the original 4-point scale, we use sliders from 0 to 100. ::: ::: {#tbl-sdo .cell tbl-cap='Social dominance orientation (SDO). Participants indicate how much they favor or oppose ideas about different social groups. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
There are many kinds of groups in the world (e.g., men and women, ethnic and religious groups, nationalities, political factions). How much do you favor or oppose the ideas about groups in general?
In setting priorities, we must consider all groups 0 = Extremely oppose … 100 = Extremely favor
We should not push for group equality 0 = Extremely oppose … 100 = Extremely favor
Group equality should be our ideal 0 = Extremely oppose … 100 = Extremely favor
Superior groups should dominate inferior groups 0 = Extremely oppose … 100 = Extremely favor
````` ::: ::: Robustness check: baseline observation carried forward (BOCF) Inverse probability weighting (IPW) solves the issue of differential attrition only under one key assumption: Conditional on pre-specified covariates, attrition is independence of potential outcomes. In other words, only if we have identified and control for all variables that cause attrition, IPW fully eliminates concerns for differential attrition. In reality, this condition is probably rarely met. While IPW can alleviate concerns of differential attrition even when not all variables are accounted for, there remains uncertainty as to how well it addresses the issue. Because of this uncertainty, we will do a robustness check using the baseline observation carried forward (BOCF) technique [@kaiserSimulatingHumanOpinions2025]. This method has been used in other influential experimental research recently [@costelloDurablyReducingConspiracy2024]. For participants with missing values for the post-treatment outcome, their pre-treatment score on that outcome gets imputed. BOCF is less suited than IPW for our study, as it requires a pre-and post-treatment measures. However, for most of our outcomes, including the main multidimensional trust in climate scientists outcome, we only have a post-measure. There are two main reason for not assessing this and most other outcome measures pre-treatment: First, several outcome measures are extensive, and assessing them twice would considerably drive up survey time, potentially resulting in survey fatigue and increased general attrition. Second, assessing the outcomes only post-treatment avoids concerns for experimenter demand effects, where participants might read the intentions of the experiment and answer in what they perceive the socially desirable way. Despite these caveats, we can use BOCF for a very conservative robustness check on regarding attrition. There is one measure which we assess both pre- and post-treatment, namely the single-item trust measure. The method comes with new assumptions, namely that for drop-out participants, the treatment score has not changed. However, this seems a conservative assumption in our study, and will, if anything, lead to underestimate the treatment effect. ### Persistence of Treatment Effects in Follow-Up Study We will test whether treatment effects observed in the main survey persist with a follow-up study, fielded one week after the end of data collection of the main study. For this analysis, we will only rely on participants who completed both the main survey and the follow-up survey. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # add identifier variables before merge # data_followup <- data_followup |> # mutate(survey = "follow_up") # # data <- data |> # mutate(survey = "main") # add demographics and pre-treatment variables to follow_up # also let all participants of main experiment appear in the data, with NAs for # those who did not fill out the survey. This is important for estimating attrition # later (and accounting for it) # data_followup <- data |> # select(id, # all_of(covariates) # ) |> # left_join(data_followup) # test # data_followup |> # summarize(sum(!is.na(trust_multidimensional) # ) # ) # merge data sets # merged_data <- data |> # bind_rows(data_followup) |> # # make `survey` a factor and set experiment as baseline # mutate( # survey = relevel(factor(survey), ref = "main") # ) ``` ::: #### Main Analysis For the main persistence analysis, we define persistence as the ability to detect a treatment effect in the followup sample. We will use the same model as for the main treatment analysis, but run it on the data from the follow-up survey. ::: {.cell} ::: We will compare the magnitude of the effects descriptively. \[make a table for change of magnitude\] #### Secondary analysis We will test whether treatment effects observed in the experiment persist with a follow-up survey, fielded one week after the end of data collection of the experiment. We will run two tests of persistence: First, we test whether treatment effects remain present in the follow-up survey. This corresponds to running the same model as for the main treatment effects in the experiment, but on the data from the follow-up survey. Second, we test whether the follow-up effects are statistically different from the main survey effects. We can run both tests from the a single interaction model, combining the data from the experiment and the follow-up survey. We will stack the main and follow-up survey data into a long-format panel dataset and estimate linear regression models including a treatment × wave interaction term. Standard errors will be clustered at the participant level to account for repeated observations across waves. $$ Y_{it} = \beta_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \beta_k \, D_{ik} + \beta_T \, Time_t + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \beta_{kT} (D_{ik} \times Time_t) + \varepsilon_{it} $$ where - $Y_{it}$ is the outcome for participant $i$ at time $t$ (main study = 0, follow-up = 1).\ - $D_{ik}$ are dummies for intervention $k$, with the main control condition as reference.\ - $Time_t$ indicates post (0) vs. follow-up (1).\ - $\beta_k$ = main study effect for reduced sample (participants who completed follow-up).\ - $\beta_k + \beta_{kT}$ = follow-up effect.\ - $\beta_{kT}$ = change in effect; statistical test of persistence. As in the experiment, we will test for attrition, and for differential attrition, in the follow-up survey. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Check baseline condition # levels(data$condition) # # # Run attrition test 1: Condition only # attrition_f_results <- map_df( # outcomes, # ~ run_attrition_f_test( # data = data_followup, # outcome = .x, # condition_var = "condition" # ) # ) # check # attrition_f_results ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # # Run attrition test 2: Condition × Covariates # attrition_interaction_results <- map_df( # outcomes, # ~ run_attrition_interactions( # data = data_followup, # outcome = .x, # covariates = covariates # ) # ) # # # check # # attrition_interaction_results ``` ::: Should we find evidence of differential attrition in the follow-up survey, we will address this issue in the same way as in the experiment: by using inverse probability of retention weights (IPW). These weights will be based on participants’ probability of completing the follow-up, conditional on the same pre-specified covariates use for IPW in the experiment. For the experimental data, weights will be the same as for the main treatment analysis weights (or no weights, depending on whether we find evidence for differential attrition, see above). Because attrition patterns may differ between the experiment and the follow-up survey---and IPW is intended to address these different patterns---a participant who completed both the experiment and the follow-up survey may receive different weights in the two data sets. ::: {.cell} ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # # check if the main control is correctly assigned to be the baseline of `condition` # levels(merged_data$condition) # # check if expeirment is correctly assigned to be the baseline of `survey` # levels(merged_data$survey) # # # example usage: compute results for all outcomes # followup_results <- map_df( # outcomes, # ~ run_followup_model( # data = data, # outcome = .x, # condition_var = "condition", # followup_var = "wave", # covariates = NULL # ) # ) ``` :::
